a1_english.title: My Daily Routine
a1_english.text: Every day, I gets up at 7:00 AM. I eats breakfast with my family. My brother like milk, but I prefers coffee. After breakfast, we goes to school by bus. The school are big and fun.

errors.explanation: Incorrect third-person -s on a first-person pronoun.
Same subject-verb agreement issue.
Missing the third-person -s for 'he'.
Incorrect -s on first-person.
Incorrect third-person plural form.
The school is singular.

a1_english.title: At the Supermarket
a1_english.text: Today, Sarah and John is at the supermarket. They wants to buy some fruits. Sarah need three apple and a banana. John look for some bread. They loves shopping together because it is easy.

errors.explanation: Plural subject needs 'are'.
Incorrect third-person plural form.
Missing third-person -s.
Missing plural -s after a number.
Missing third-person -s.
Incorrect -s on plural subject.

a1_english.title: My Favorite Pet
a1_english.text: I have a dog. He name is Max. Max are two years old. He have beautiful brown hair and a short tail. Max like to run in the park on weekends. I love he very much.

errors.explanation: Using a subject pronoun instead of a possessive adjective.
Max is singular.
Irregular third-person singular form of 'to have'.
Missing third-person -s.
Using a subject pronoun instead of an object pronoun.

a2_english.title: Last Weekend
a2_english.text: Last Saturday, I go to the beach with my friends. The weather was very hot, so we swimmed in the sea for hours. At lunch, we eated delicious sandwiches. In evening, we watched the sunset. It was more better than staying at home.

errors.explanation: Past tense required for 'Last Saturday'.
Incorrect regular past tense for the irregular verb 'swim'.
Incorrect regular past tense for the irregular verb 'eat'.
Missing the definite article in the time phrase.
Double comparative; 'better' is already the comparative form of 'good'.

a2_english.title: Planning a Trip
a2_english.text: Next month, my family is going to visit London. We are exciting because we never went there before. My mother wants to see the Big Ben, but my father don't like crowded places. I think London is beautifuler than my hometown, so I can't wait.

errors.explanation: -ed vs -ing adjective; the family feels excited, the trip itself is exciting.
Third-person singular negative requires 'doesn't'.
Do not use 'the' before specific names of famous monuments/buildings like Big Ben.
Long adjectives take 'more' instead of '-er' for comparatives.

a2_english.title: My Cooking Hobby
a2_english.text: I love cooking on my kitchen. I can to cook many different dishes, like pasta and pizza. Yesterday, I tried a new recipe, but I made it too quickly and it tasted badly. My brother said I should practicing more if I want to be a chef.

errors.explanation: Incorrect preposition of place.
Modal verbs like 'can' are followed by the bare infinitive, without 'to'.
Verbs of sense like taste, smell, and look take adjectives, not adverbs, to describe the subject.
The modal verb 'should' must be followed by the base form of the verb.

b1_english.title: The New Job
b1_english.text: I have started my new job three weeks ago. I work as an assistant in a big company who designs software. I am very interested on this field, but the work is hard. If I will work overtime this week, my manager will give me a bonus next Monday.

errors.explanation: The present perfect cannot be used with a specific past time marker like 'three weeks ago'; use Past Simple.
Relative pronoun 'who' is only for people; companies take 'which' or 'that'.
Incorrect dependent preposition; the correct collocation is always 'interested in'.
First Conditional rule: the 'if' clause uses the present simple, while the result clause uses 'will'.

b1_english.title: A Holiday in Rome
b1_english.text: I have lived in this city since five years, but I never visited Italy until last summer. While I walked down a narrow street in Rome, I suddenly saw a beautiful hidden fountain. Many photos were took by me because the water looked so clear.

errors.explanation: 'Since' is used for a specific starting point in time; 'for' is used for a duration/length of time.
Past Continuous is needed here to show an ongoing action that was interrupted by a sudden event.
In the passive voice, the past participle 'taken' must be used after the verb 'to be,' not the past simple 'took'.

b1_english.title: Learning English
b1_english.text: Learning a new language is difficult, but it depends of how much you practice. I have been studying English for two years, and my speaking skills became much better. If you don't practice every day, you forgot new vocabulary quickly.

errors.explanation: Incorrect dependent preposition; the verb 'depend' always pairs with 'on'.
Because the action started in the past and relates to the present, the Present Perfect is needed.
This is a Zero Conditional stating a general truth/fact. Both clauses must be in the Present Simple.

b1plus_english.title: Changing Careers
b1plus_english.text: By the time Mark turned thirty, he decided to change his career. He used to working as an accountant, but he hated sitting at a desk all day. If he had more free time, he would learn how to code, but his current job keeps him too busy. His friend suggested him to take an online course instead.

errors.explanation: Past Perfect is required here because the decision happened before the specific past time marker of turning thirty.
'Used to' for past habits/states is followed by the infinitive.

errors.correction: suggested that he take / suggested taking
errors.explanation: The verb 'suggest' cannot be followed by an object pronoun + to-infinitive.

b1plus_english.title: The Interview
b1plus_english.text: Yesterday, Sarah went to an interview for a graphic designer position. The manager asked her how long she has been designing logos. Sarah replied that she was working in marketing since 2022. She managed impressing the panel, so they promised calling her by the end of the week.

errors.explanation: Reported speech rule: the present perfect 'has been' must backshift to the past perfect 'had been'.
Because of the time marker 'since 2022' in a past context/reported speech, the Past Perfect Continuous is required.
The verb 'manage' must be followed by a to-infinitive, not a gerund.
The verb 'promise' also requires a to-infinitive.

b1plus_english.title: Overcoming Fears
b1plus_english.text: When I was younger, I would be afraid of public speaking. My teacher told me that if I practiced in front of a mirror, I will feel more confident. I avoided to give presentations for years, but last week I finally delivered a speech without reading my notes.

errors.explanation: While 'would' can describe past habits, it cannot be used with stative verbs like 'be' to describe past states.
This is reported/indirect speech of a conditional statement; 'will' must backshift to 'would'.
The verb 'avoid' must be followed by a gerund, not a to-infinitive.

b2_english.title: A Missed Opportunity
b2_english.text: If Alex would have studied harder last semester, he would be at university right now instead of retaking his exams. He feels like he wasted his time. By this time next year, however, he will graduate and can finally start his career. Little he knew that the final exam would be so intensely difficult.

errors.explanation: Third/Mixed Conditional rule: the 'if' clause takes the Past Perfect, never 'would have'.
The future time marker 'By this time next year' requires the Future Perfect to show an action that will be completed by a certain point in the future.
Negative/restrictive adverbials like 'Little' at the beginning of a sentence require subject-verb inversion.

b2_english.title: The Project Deadline
b2_english.text: The team was exhausted because they had been working around the clock. The project should be finished by Friday, but a massive server crash delayed everything. Jessica must leave her laptop at the office yesterday because she didn't have it when she got home. Having realizing they were out of time, they asked for an extension.

errors.explanation: To express an unfulfilled obligation or expectation in the past, you must use a past modal structure: should + have + past participle.
For strong logical deductions about a past event, use must have + past participle.
Perfect participle clauses require Having + past participle to show that one action happened before another.

b2_english.title: Urban Development
b2_english.text: The city council is planning to build a new highway. Not only the project will cost millions of dollars, but it will also displace dozens of families. Many residents object against the plan, claiming that public transit should be prioritized. The mayor, whom has been under immense pressure, refused to comment on the matter.

errors.explanation: When 'Not only' begins a sentence, it triggers an inversion of the subject and the auxiliary verb.
Incorrect dependent preposition; the correct collocation is always to object to something.
The relative pronoun acts as the subject of the relative clause, so the subject pronoun 'who' must be used instead of the object pronoun 'whom'.

b2plus_english.title: The Corporate Crisis
b2plus_english.text: The CEO demanded that the marketing director resigns immediately following the PR disaster. Under no circumstances employees are allowed to speak to the press without prior clearance. Had the company responded sooner, they might not be facing such a severe backlash now. The board is currently considering to hire an external crisis management firm.

errors.explanation: The verb 'demand' triggers the subjunctive mood in a that-clause, requiring the bare/base form of the verb, regardless of the subject.
Negative adverbial phrases at the start of a sentence require subject-auxiliary inversion.
The verb 'consider' must be followed by a gerund, not a to-infinitive.

b2plus_english.title: Literary Criticisms
b2plus_english.text: Hardly had the controversial novel been published when critics started tearing it apart. The author wishes she didn't write it so hastily, as the plot holes are glaringly obvious. I look forward to read her next book, though, because despite of her weak plotting, her prose style is absolutely mesmerizing. She really needs to have her work edit by a professional.

errors.explanation: To express regret about a past action, 'wish' must be followed by the Past Perfect, not the Past Simple.
In the phrase 'look forward to,' the word 'to' acts as a preposition, which means it must be followed by a gerund.
The preposition 'despite' is never followed by 'of'.
The causative structure have + object + past participle is required here to show an action done by someone else.

b2plus_english.title: Eco-Tourism Debate
b2plus_english.text: The small island has seen a massive influx of tourists, that has severely threatened the local ecosystem. The local government is accused of prioritizing profit over sustainability. It is high time they take drastic measures to limit visitors. Unless stricter regulations are implemented, irreversible damage will be done to the coral reefs, resulting in the island losing its primary appeal.

errors.explanation: In non-defining relative clauses, you must use 'which' instead of 'that' to refer to the whole situation.
The expression 'It is high time' is a hypothetical structure that requires the Unreal Past / Past Simple to express urgency about a present action.
